Angkor Wat is a Hindu temple complex at Angkor, Cambodia, built for the king Suryavarman II in the early 12th century as his state temple and part of his capital city. (The Angkorian period dates 802-1432). As the best-preserved temple  ...    temple mountain and the later galleried temple, based on early South Indian Hindu architecture. It is designed to represent Mount Meru, home of the devas in Hindu mythology: within a moat and an outer wall 3.6 kilometres (2.2 mi) long are three rectangular galleries, each raised above the next. At the centre of the temple stands a quincunx of towers. Unlike most Angkorian temples, Angkor Wat is oriented to the west. A UNESCO World Heritage Site.  ...    ## Angkor Wat: Center axial

title: Mount Meru in Buddhism: The Cosmic Axis of the Universe
highlights: Meru has profoundly influenced Buddhist sacred art, iconography, and temple design—especially in Southeast Asia. ### ✦ Angkor Wat: A Temple-Mountain Perhaps the most spectacular example is Angkor Wat in Cambodia. Built in the 12th century by King Suryavarman II, this vast complex is designed as a terrestrial representation of Mount Meru: Its central quincunx of towers represents the five peaks of Meru. The moat symbolizes the cosmic ocean surrounding the mountain. The temple rises in successive tiers, reflecting the ascent from the mundane to the divine. This concept of the temple-mountain is echoed in many Khmer and Thai temples, such as Bayon, Borobudur, and Wat Arun,  ...    In Khmer art, Devas (gods) and Asuras (demons) are often depicted churning the Ocean of Milk around Mount Mandara

title: The Sacred Alignment of Angkor Wat
highlights: a map of the cosmos, a calendar in  ...    orientation plays a crucial role in the temple’s astronomical alignments. The temple’s design embodies Mount Meru, the mythical mountain at the center of the Hindu universe, surrounded by oceans and concentric continents. Its soaring towers represent the peaks of Meru, while the moats symbolize the cosmic seas. The Khmer architects envisioned Angkor Wat not simply as a temple, but as the universe itself carved in stone.  ...    reflects a deep awareness of mathematics, geometry, and astronomy. Water reflected the temple and the sky, doubling the image of Angkor Wat and creating a mirror of the cosmos. At sunrise, the reflection of the central tower rising with the sun would have created a powerful vision of unity between heaven and earth.
